title: Distillation
description: A teacher-student training regime where a smaller model learns from supervision produced by a larger or stronger teacher model.
openingSummary: Large models can be strong teachers but expensive to run at scale. Distillation trains a smaller student model to imitate teacher behavior so teams can deploy a cheaper model that keeps much of the teacher's useful output quality.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Distillation is a training regime built around two models: a teacher that provides supervision and a student that learns from that supervision. The student is usually smaller or cheaper to run than the teacher.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: Teams use distillation when they want a model that is easier to serve in production without giving up behavior they already trust from a stronger teacher.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The teacher produces training signal for the student, the student is updated on that signal, and the result is a model sized for deployment rather than for maximum training-time capacity.

comparedToNearbyRegimes.title: Compared To Nearby Regimes
comparedToNearbyRegimes.body: Distillation is broader than any one variant. Some routines use fixed offline teacher outputs, while others refresh supervision from behavior produced under the current student policy.

limitationsAndFailureModes.title: Limitations And Failure Modes
limitationsAndFailureModes.body: A student can only recover what the teacher signal actually covers. Weak supervision, narrow training data, or a student that is too small for the target behavior can all leave capability gaps.

trainingFlow.title: Distillation training flow
trainingFlow.alt: A flow from teacher model to training supervision to student update and deployment-oriented student output.
trainingFlow.caption: Distillation moves teacher supervision into a smaller student model sized for deployment.
